Scenario.title: SPACEHOP Cyber Intrusion in Energy Sector
Scenario.overview: Energy sector organizations Complex This scenario outlines a multi-stage cyber attack attributed to the SPACEHOP activity campaign, leveraging basic attacker skills to compromise systems through reconnaissance, resource development, and persistent lateral movement within the energy industry infrastructure.

threat_actor_profile.motivation: Gather sensitive data or disrupt operations for potential financial or strategic benefit.

Scenario.attack_surface: AVAILABLE_SURFACES: Internet-facing assets, Operational systems, User endpoints, Network infrastructure, Shared resources
Scenario.attack_vector: AVAILABLE_VECTORS: Social engineering, Malware delivery, Proxy usage, Network scanning, Credential stuffing, Stolen credentials

stage.name: Reconnaissance
stage.description: An attacker uses artificial intelligence tools to analyze publicly available data sources, such as corporate websites or job listings, to identify potential targets and gather preliminary information on system configurations or employee roles relevant to the energy sector.

stage.name: Resource Development
stage.description: The attacker configures or exploits leased virtual private servers (VPS) and operationally relayed box (ORB) networks to anonymize further attack activities, facilitating covert operations within the energy environment.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.kev: CVE-2024-27198: Authentication bypass vulnerability in JetBrains TeamCity allowing admin actions
stage.description: By sending simple phishing emails to employees, the attacker tricks them into opening malicious attachments or links, leading to credential disclosure or execution of malware that establishes an initial foothold on internal systems.

stage.name: Execution
stage.kev: CVE-2025-24472: Authentication bypass vulnerability in Fortinet FortiOS/FortiProxy granting super-admin privileges
stage.capec: CAPEC-9: Buffer Overflow in Local Command-Line Utilities
stage.description: Once initial access is obtained, the attacker runs malicious scripts or commands via built-in utilities like PowerShell or cmd.exe to execute unauthorized processes, potentially exploiting known vulnerabilities to gain deeper control over the compromised endpoint.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.description: Malicious code is added to modify registry settings or create scheduled tasks to ensure the attacker maintains access even after system reboots or routine maintenance in the energy sector's operational technology (OT) environment.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.description: Using basic cracking tools or social engineering, the attacker attempts to guess or brute-force common passwords associated with user accounts already accessed, elevating privileges to administrative levels for greater control over energy-related systems.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.description: To avoid detection, logs are deleted or modified using simple scripting, obscuring the attacker's tracks in the energy organization's monitoring systems and reducing the chances of alerting security teams.

stage.name: Credential Access
stage.description: Via brute-forcing techniques, the attacker systematically tests username-password combinations on accessible operational database interfaces within the energy sector, aiming to harvest additional login credentials for expanded access.

stage.name: Discovery
stage.description: Open ports and active hosts are identified through network scans, possibly using hidden proxy hops, to map out the internal energy network structure and locate valuable assets or data points that can be targeted next.

stage.name: Lateral Movement
stage.description: With stolen credentials, the attacker moves laterally across the energy network by querying SMB shares or other file-based protocols to access more systems, escalating the scope of the intrusion beyond the initial entry point.

stage.name: Collection
stage.capec: CAPEC-675: Retrieve Data from Decommissioned Devices
stage.description: Sensitive files containing proprietary information, employee details, or system configurations are copied from targeted energy systems, often deleting originals to minimize forensic evidence left behind during exfiltration preparation.

stage.name: Exfiltration
stage.capec: CAPEC-636: Hiding Malicious Data or Code within Files
stage.description: Collected data is concealed within seemingly normal files, such as images or PDFs, using steganographic methods, and transferred out via encrypted channels or disguised emails sent through proxy networks to obscure the origin in the energy sector.

stage.name: Impact
stage.description: A denial-of-service attack is launched by overwhelming network traffic or disrupting communication protocols, specifically targeting critical energy systems like SCADA controllers or web servers to degrade or halt essential operations intentionally.

Scenario.summary: The SPACEHOP campaign demonstrates a coordinated attack starting with basic reconnaissance and phishing, progressing through execution and persistence to achieve widespread lateral movement and data collection within the energy industry, culminating in disruptive impacts aimed at compromising organizational stability.
